title: What is a Decision Tree
content: A Decision Tree is a supervised machine learning algorithm used for both classification and regression tasks. It predicts the target variable by recursively splitting the dataset into smaller subsets based on feature values. Each internal node represents a decision based on a feature, each branch represents the outcome of that decision, and each leaf node represents the final prediction. Decision Trees are highly interpretable and closely resemble human decision-making processes.

title: Historical Background
content: Decision Trees originated from statistical decision theory and became popular because they produce models that are easy to understand and visualize. Algorithms such as ID3, C4.5, C5.0, and CART introduced efficient methods for constructing decision trees using information gain and impurity measures. Today, Decision Trees form the foundation of many ensemble learning methods including Random Forest and Gradient Boosting.

title: Real-world Applications
content: Decision Trees are used in credit risk analysis, medical diagnosis, fraud detection, customer segmentation, loan approval, recommendation systems, manufacturing quality control, and predictive maintenance. Their interpretability makes them popular in domains where understanding the reasoning behind predictions is important.

title: Components of a Decision Tree
content: A Decision Tree consists of a root node, internal decision nodes, branches, and leaf nodes. The root node contains the entire dataset. Internal nodes perform feature-based tests. Branches represent the outcome of those tests, while leaf nodes contain the predicted class or regression value. Prediction is performed by traversing the tree from the root to a leaf based on feature values.

title: Splitting Criteria
content: Decision Trees determine the best feature for splitting the data by measuring impurity reduction. Common splitting criteria include Gini Impurity, Information Gain based on Entropy, and Log Loss. Regression trees typically minimize Mean Squared Error or Mean Absolute Error. The feature producing the greatest reduction in impurity is selected for splitting.

title: Tree Growth and Pruning
content: Decision Trees are built recursively using a greedy algorithm. At every node, the algorithm selects the split that maximizes impurity reduction. If allowed to grow without restrictions, Decision Trees tend to overfit the training data. Pruning techniques remove unnecessary branches to improve generalization. Pre-pruning limits tree growth using parameters such as maximum depth, while post-pruning removes weak branches after the tree has been constructed.

title: Important Hyperparameters
content: Important Decision Tree hyperparameters include criterion, splitter, max_depth, min_samples_split, min_samples_leaf, max_features, max_leaf_nodes, min_impurity_decrease, ccp_alpha, and random_state. Proper tuning of these parameters helps reduce overfitting while maintaining predictive performance.

title: Model Characteristics
content: Decision Trees require very few assumptions about the underlying data. They can model nonlinear relationships, handle numerical and categorical features, and automatically capture feature interactions. However, they are sensitive to noisy data and small changes in the training dataset may produce significantly different tree structures.

title: Evaluation Metrics
content: Classification Decision Trees are evaluated using Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1 Score, ROC-AUC, and Confusion Matrix. Regression Decision Trees are evaluated using Mean Squared Error, Mean Absolute Error, Root Mean Squared Error, and R² Score. Cross-validation is commonly used to estimate generalization performance.

title: scikit-learn Usage
content:
Typical implementation:

from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier

model = DecisionTreeClassifier(
    criterion='gini',
    max_depth=5,
    random_state=42
)

model.fit(X_train, y_train)

predictions = model.predict(X_test)

Important constructor parameters include criterion, splitter, max_depth, min_samples_split, min_samples_leaf, max_features, random_state, max_leaf_nodes, ccp_alpha, and class_weight. Useful attributes include feature_importances_, tree_, classes_, n_features_in_, and feature_names_in_.

title: Advantages and Limitations
content: Decision Trees are easy to understand, require little data preprocessing, handle missing values reasonably well, support nonlinear decision boundaries, and provide feature importance estimates. Their major limitation is overfitting, especially when trees become very deep. They are also unstable because small changes in the data may produce different tree structures.

title: Decision Tree vs Random Forest
content: A Decision Tree builds a single tree to make predictions, whereas Random Forest combines predictions from many independently trained trees. Decision Trees are easier to interpret but are more prone to overfitting. Random Forest generally achieves higher predictive accuracy and better generalization by averaging multiple trees, although it sacrifices interpretability and requires more computational resources.
